data.title: Despite Starmer’s ‘China threat’ rhetoric, Britain seeks tighter ties with Beijing
data.description:
British Prime Minister Keir Starmer called China a security threat this week, but analysts said the statement was motivated by domestic politics and did not negate London’s goal of deepening economic ties with Beijing.
Meanwhile, disputes around a new Chinese embassy will continue to test this desire for stronger relations as the British leader plans a state visit.
Starmer said on Monday that his government would focus on the relationship with China, which would help protect national interests, while recognising the “reality” that China posed “real national security threats” to the United Kingdom.
“Protecting our security is non-negotiable. Our first duty,” Starmer said at a banquet traditionally used to set out foreign policy for the year ahead.
“But by taking tough steps to keep us secure, we enable ourselves to cooperate in other areas.”
The prime minister’s statement was the latest in a series of assertions about China being a security threat. These statements have received strong pushback from Beijing, which urged London to adopt a more constructive and accurate view of bilateral relations.
On Tuesday, Britain delayed for a third time its decision on China’s contentious plan to build a massive new embassy in London. Critics fear the project, which would be the largest embassy in Europe, could serve as a base for espionage.
Last month, Britain’s domestic spy service, MI5, warned lawmakers that Chinese intelligence services were posing as recruiters to target people working in parliament. In a rare move, the agency identified two LinkedIn profiles under the names Amanda Qiu and Shirly Shen as being linked to Chinese intelligence.
In October, prosecutors abruptly abandoned a case against two British men charged with spying for Beijing, claiming that the government’s evidence was missing a “critical element”.
The Chinese embassy has repeatedly denied the espionage accusations, calling them “pure fabrication”.
It has also rejected Starmer’s “erroneous” characterisation of a “China threat”, asserting instead that China’s development would only bring opportunities globally.
“The UK should have a clear understanding of the situation, adjust its mindset, adopt a rational and friendly approach towards China’s development, pursue a positive and pragmatic policy towards China, and work with China in the same direction to steer China-UK relations onto a path of sound and steady development,” the embassy said on Tuesday.


Observers noted that Starmer’s choice of words was not surprising and mainly served a domestic purpose.
“There are things that both sides find it important to say in public. But I think it does not affect the ongoing substance of the [bilateral] dialogue,” said Andrew Cainey, senior associate fellow at the Royal United Services Institute.
Tim Summers, an assistant professor at the Chinese University of Hong Kong’s Centre for China Studies, said domestic politics was the main reason Starmer made the speech.
“He’s feeling weak and insecure, and therefore, by saying this, he tries to fend off criticism from those, and there’ll be many of them, who will criticise him simply for going to China,” Summers said.
That was echoed by Zeng Jinghan, an international affairs professor at City University of Hong Kong.
“The real focus of the issue now is whether the so-called threat has escalated to a state-level concern – to the point where China could be defined as an adversary,” Zeng said.
“As long as Starmer does not do so, all the other rhetoric is merely a form of appeasement, a play on words,” he said.
Despite internal divisions over Britain’s China policy, the ruling Labour Party still aims to advance a distinct approach and re-engage with Beijing – a goal it has maintained since taking office last July and reiterated in Starmer’s recent speech.
At the Monday event, Starmer said Britain’s policy towards China could not continue to blow “hot and cold”, and failing to navigate bilateral ties would be “dereliction of duty”.
“We had the golden age of relations … which then flipped to an Ice Age, that some still advocate.”
“It’s time for a serious approach, to reject the simplistic binary choice,” he said, stressing that the scale of the opportunity in China was “immense”.
The prime minister plans to visit Beijing and Shanghai in late January, with a trip tentatively scheduled for January 29 to 31, though the dates have not been finalised, according to Bloomberg, citing sources familiar with the matter.
If confirmed, Starmer would be the first British prime minister to visit China in seven years.
Ties between London and Beijing have deteriorated significantly over the crackdown on anti-government protests in Hong Kong, allegations of espionage and cyberattacks, and Beijing’s close relations with Russia.


The approval of the new Chinese embassy in London stands as a key symbolic test of the British government’s commitment to improving relations with China, according to observers.
“If London can approve the plan, the presence of a Chinese embassy there would instil strong confidence in many Chinese enterprises,” Zeng said.
It also signals that the Labour government is capable of withstanding domestic political pressure and strongly supporting China.
“The embassy itself is an anchor. For the UK, the fundamental issue remains its economic and trade relationship with China, but whether the embassy is approved or not can determine whether these economic ties have a firm anchor in place,” he said.
China’s plans for a new 20,000-square-metre (215,278 sq ft) embassy near the Tower of London have stalled for the past three years due to opposition.
Beijing decried the repeated delays on Tuesday, urging Britain to approve the plans as soon as possible to “avoid further harming mutual trust and bilateral cooperation between the two sides”.
The same day, the prime minister’s spokesman said the plans could bring “security advantages”. The government is expected to approve the project in January.
Steve Tsang, director of the China Institute at the School of Oriental and African Studies, University of London, said Britain’s biggest domestic challenge was balancing national security concerns with positive engagement.
He added that the other challenge was Beijing’s attitude, as reaffirmed by the embassy’s statement, which essentially said that London should accept Beijing’s interpretation to mend ties.
“It takes two to tango, and Starmer willing to dance will not get very far if Beijing insists on conditions before dancing.”
“Beijing clearly wants to drive the relationship and wants the UK to be useful to China. Starmer has not so far demonstrated an understanding of it, but he may get a taste of it when he visits Beijing, particularly if a positive decision for the new Chinese mega embassy has not yet been approved by then.”
According to Zeng, Beijing’s core demand from London is its neutrality amid the ongoing rivalry between China and the United States.
He said this would be difficult given the historically strong alliance between Britain and the US, but Trump’s election had created some space as his relationship with the incumbent Labour Party was not as close as it used to be.
Cainey of the Royal United Services Institute said Starmer was likely to pursue a pragmatic partnership with China on shared challenges such as climate, global health and artificial intelligence (AI) governance.
He cautioned that implementation remained the key hurdle.
“The UK’s balanced approach towards China has been there for a while. What has not been there is the consistent execution through meetings, engagement, and through specific guidance to companies and universities on how to work productively and securely with China.”
Additional reporting by Zhao Ziwen

data.title: Hong Kong firefighters describe rescue efforts during Tai Po estate blaze
data.description:
Firefighters have described the gruelling conditions they faced in their search for survivors of Hong Kong’s worst fire in more than seven decades, with rescuers climbing dozens of flights of stairs as they conserved oxygen to look for anyone alive on the higher floors.
For some firefighters, the operation has left them with feelings of guilt and helplessness after they only found bodies. One said encountering the scattered corpses had given him nightmares that left him unable to sleep normally for days, but he remained committed to his job.
The firefighters have been posting their accounts on the social media platform Threads over the past week, recounting the extreme challenges inside the Wang Fuk Court residential complex in Tai Po that caught fire on November 26.
At least 159 people died in the blaze, including a firefighter. Seven of the eight 31-storey towers were destroyed.
“Even though on the internet, many firefighters describe rescuing many survivors, I am in the darkest corner [mentally]. That’s because all I rescued were bodies,” one user who identified himself as “TTT” wrote.

Another firefighter wrote that he and his teammates had to walk up to flats on the 20th floor and above to look for survivors, and he would ration the amount of oxygen available in the tank he carried to maximise the search time.
“Each time, I would don my mask and switch on the oxygen supply when I arrived at the 10th floor or above,” he wrote. “But even then, there was not much time left to work on the targeted [upper] floors.”
At one point last Thursday, scaffolding fell at the exact spot where he was standing only moments earlier, he said.
“The situation was so dire on the second day, one can only imagine how tough and dangerous it was for the brothers on the first day,” he wrote.
The flames were finally extinguished on Friday and the search for survivors ended on Saturday. Firefighters continued to carry out investigative work inside the towers as members of the police force’s Disaster Victim Identification Unit searched for bodies in an operation that ended this past Wednesday.
A firefighter on his third shift on Sunday said that the higher he went inside Wang Cheong House, the first building that caught fire, the more cordoned off areas he saw.
The areas were where bodies lay.
“I just thought, if we had arrived earlier, could we have brought you away from the fire? I know, it’s difficult, really difficult!” he wrote.
“Just from the surroundings, one can imagine the high temperatures then.”

The firefighter who spoke of scattered bodies said he could not sleep properly for two days after his shift, and he would experience flashbacks and wake up from nightmares.
Anthony Lam Chun-man, former director of fire services, explained to the Post that firefighters would face dense smoke and the physical toll of carrying heavy equipment.
Firefighters responding to an emergency at a building would first determine whether fire equipment or emergency lifts were usable and resort to carrying a fire hosepipe up floors if no facilities were available, he said.
“The firefighting equipment weighs around 30 to 40 pounds [13 to 18kg]. That includes the protective suit, boots, a hat, an axe and the oxygen tank,” Lam said.
They would also have to watch out for falling debris in an environment filled with dense smoke, he added.
“You can’t see your fingers from your outstretched hand [amid the smoke]. Taking each step forward requires exploring by touch,” Lam said.
The former fire chief added that firefighters’ protective gear, while effective, could not cover every inch of skin, leaving areas such as the neck more exposed to the extreme heat.

Lam, who helped battle the blaze at the Blue Box Industrial Building in Aberdeen in 1984, which burned for 68 hours and was classified as a No 5 alarm fire, the most severe, described his experience as “extreme”.
His shift lasted for 12 hours, where he swapped between firefighting and being on standby.
“Carrying those 40 pounds of equipment inside, how long can you work? Once you come out, another team enters. You rest for a while and then go back in again,” Lam said.
But the fire chief said he would channel the stress and concerns over safety into motivation to rescue more survivors.
“TTT”, who wrote of flashbacks of scattered bodies, said he was not deterred by the trauma.
“I can only say, after experiencing this No 5 alarm, I’ve realised I truly love my job,” he wrote. “[It’s] because this job is to help people. I’ve never thought there’s any heroism attached to me.”

data.title: Flat owners warned as consultant on fire-hit Hong Kong estate signals exit
data.description:
The consultant behind the renovation of Wang Fuk Court, the housing estate reduced to ruins in Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in seven decades, is to close down as experts warn the case highlights limited protection for flat owners under the city’s corporate liability laws.
The Hong Kong Association of Property Management Companies told the Post on Wednesday that some of its members had been notified of the closure of Will Power Architects, the consultancy overseeing HK$336 million (US$43.18 million) renovation project at the Tai Po estate.
News of the closure spread after its two directors were among 21 people arrested in connection with the fire that swept through seven of eight blocks of the estate and left 159 dead.
Johnnie Chan Chi-kau, past president of the association, said some management companies in Hong Kong had received “oral notifications” about the shutdown while some property owners reported receiving written notices.
However, he added that the association itself had not been formally notified by the consultancy.
“In these circumstances, it is essential for the owners’ corporation and property management companies to seek legal and technical advice to effectively manage subsequent arrangements,” the association said.
Will Power Architects was a central player in the project, along with main contractor Prestige Construction and Engineering, property manager ISS EastPoint, and the estate’s owners’ corporation.
Subcontractors such as Victory Fire Engineering were responsible for maintaining the estate’s fire safety systems.
Labour Department records show that Prestige had previously been convicted of safety offences.
Yet, a progress report for the renovation project – submitted by Will Power and reviewed and signed by its directors Wong Hap-yin and Ng Yeuk – explicitly certified that the contractor, Prestige, had neither litigation records with owners nor any convictions by the department.
Notably, Prestige received an “A” rating during the tender assessment for its HK$152 million bid, which was the closest to an independent cost estimate of HK$144 million provided by the Urban Renewal Authority, and a HK$1 million contingency fund for underground piping.
According to the website of The Hong Kong Institute of Architects, Will Power is a registered practice, while director Ng has been a member since May 2009.
The Post has contacted the institute for comment.

Solicitor and lawmaker Doreen Kong Yuk-foon said the sudden closure of the consultancy could leave owners facing “unfinished projects” and little hope of compensation, as the corporate veil often protected directors from civil liability.
“If a company is the contracting party and it has now closed down, theoretically, its directors and shareholders will not bear [civil] liability,” Kong said. “Often, this becomes a trap... they can just walk away, and you cannot pursue them.”
She added that many such firms operate with low share capital, making litigation futile.
“I saw that its [share capital] base is very small – probably only HK$100,000,” she said. “What can you sue them for? Actually, this is terrible. Even if they have money, or haven’t closed, it takes time and resources for an owners’ corporation to sue.”
Kong urged the government to establish a dedicated task force to assist affected owners of unfinished projects to find replacement consultants and handle enquiries, noting that the complexity and cost of litigation often discouraged legal action.
“For an owners’ corporation to sue someone, they first have to hold meetings to get consensus from owners, pay huge legal fees, and everyone will have to chip in,” Kong said. “This difficulty leads many to feel that doing work of owners’ corporations is burdensome…and often being manipulated by unscrupulous contractors.”
She also highlighted the procedural hurdles in claiming professional indemnity insurance, which required first proving negligence in court, a lengthy process that, she said, underscored the need for systemic reform such as class action mechanisms.
“You have to first determine if there was negligence... you cannot go directly to the professional bodies to claim for professional indemnity,” Kong said. “Many litigations will be generated. Please, government, make [a] law enabling class action lawsuits as soon as possible, and at least for Wang Fuk Court owners and victims.”
Nathan Lee, chairman of the Hong Kong Institute of Surveyors’ Building Surveying Division, said that while consultancy contracts typically included termination clauses for non-performance or negligence, claiming damages from a collapsing firm was often extremely difficult.
“If it can be proven that the resigning consultant company committed misconduct or neglect, the owners’ corporation can, in accordance with the contract, seek to recover relevant expenses and losses, such as the cost difference for hiring a new consultant,” Lee said.
However, he cautioned that the legal reality often favoured the defunct company if it lacked assets.
“The success of such claims depends on the company’s registration status – whether it is a sole proprietorship or an unlimited or a limited company – and its debt priority,” Lee said. “The worst-case scenario is if the company collapses, becomes insolvent, or has very low registered capital; the chance of successfully recovering costs is relatively low.”
He added that any recovery would depend heavily on the firm’s “debt priority”, if any, meaning owners would likely find themselves at the back of the queue behind other creditors.
Will Power had no outstanding bankruptcy petitions as of Thursday evening, according to an online search of Official Receiver’s Office records by the Post.
The Post has reached out to Will Power and Prestige for comment.

data.title: Macron urges Chinese firms to invest in France, EU as tech gap widens
data.description:
French President Emmanuel Macron has called on Chinese companies to invest in France and transfer know-how in emerging sectors like batteries, electric vehicles (EVs) and solar panels – highlighting growing concerns over the widening technological gap between the two countries.
“We want to welcome more Chinese investment through projects and re-industrialisation strategies, and this is what will allow us to break the cycle we’re currently in,” he said on Thursday in Beijing, where he attended a French-Chinese Business Council meeting alongside President Xi Jinping.
Macron called for “mutually beneficial projects” in sectors where he acknowledged China’s technological advantage, and urged Chinese firms to play a role in Europe’s development.
Chinese customs data on last year’s top traded goods with France could shed light on Paris’ motivations. Eight of China’s 10 largest exports to France by value were technologically advanced goods. EV batteries, laptops, solar panels and turbojet engine components featured in the top five, though small parcels still topped the list, mainly from e-commerce platforms like Shein and Temu.
The remainder of the top ten included container ships, electronic equipment, data processors, smartphones and clothes.
By contrast, four of France’s top exports to China by value were critical technologies, including aircraft engines, medicine and medium- to large-sized aircraft. Cosmetic products topped the list, accounting for 9 per cent of China’s total import value from the European country. The rest were fashion and agricultural goods such as cognac, handbags, flax, jewellery and wheat.
Analysts said the data pointed to a reversal in trade dynamics. Twenty years ago, China mainly sold basic consumer goods like clothes, tables and chairs to France, while the European country led in high-speed rail and aerospace technology, said Sacha Courtial, a China researcher at the Paris-based Institut Jacques Delors think tank.


“We had the fastest train in the world, the fastest plane in the world, the best nuclear power plant in the world … now what we have is L’Oreal.”
He acknowledged the appeal of French fashion and luxury brands in China as a positive, but warned these products are far more easily replaceable than advanced technologies. Chinese consumer preferences could shift overnight, seriously undermining market share, he said, noting that demand for hi-tech products such as aeroplanes tends to be more stable.
In response to this shift, France is eager to attract Chinese advanced technologies through investment, said an Elysee Palace source during a briefing on November 27. Echoing this, Philippe Le Corre, head of the Asia programme at French business school ESSEC, called on China to undergo a “reverse Deng Xiaoping” by making major investments in both France and Europe.
But given the lack of trust between Europe and China, fuelled in part by Beijing’s stance on the war in Ukraine and other issues, Brussels might have to force technology transfers by restricting market access, he said.
As Europe’s second-largest economy, France has advocated a more protectionist trade strategy, occasionally clashing with Germany. Paris backed the EU’s anti-subsidy tariffs on Chinese EVs, a major point of contention.
In this new era of global competition, Europe must stay competitive and innovative, offering products that continue to appeal to Chinese buyers, said Courtial of the Institut Jacques Delors.
“There’s a lot that we can learn from Chinese technologies right now – that’s why we need to keep a partnership on different topics.”
Additional reporting by Mia Nurmamat

data.title: Can China fund its AI ambitions? Ex-Google CEO Eric Schmidt casts doubt
data.description:
China’s designs on supremacy in artificial intelligence could be hindered by a funding gap in the sector, according to former Google CEO Eric Schmidt, who gave a grim assessment of the country’s AI prospects as China and the US lock horns for dominance.
However, analysts said that funding would not be a concern for China’s AI development given the deep pockets of the country’s private and state investors – particularly as Beijing, Big Tech firms and investors were united in their determination to accelerate AI development from chips to applications.
Schmidt said that compared with the US, which had the world’s “most extraordinary capital market”, China lacked the same “depth of the financial market”, adding that Chinese start-ups literally “cannot get the money” to keep up in the AI race.
“Without that access to capital, it’s very hard to do these large models with this complicated training,” Schmidt said at a forum hosted by the Harvard Kennedy School on Monday, according to a video published on the school’s YouTube channel.
Schmidt’s remarks reflected how a challenging Chinese economy and evolving start-up scene had already dampened the appetite of market-driven venture capital funds in recent years.
This trend has since been compounded by US restrictions that have prevented venture capital funds – historically among the largest sources of capital making lucrative bets on China’s tech and internet growth – from backing key Chinese sectors including AI, citing national security concerns.
However, capital from the government and Big Tech firms had flowed in to make up for the lack of market-driven venture capital, according to analysts at PitchBook, a global start-up and venture capital data service firm.
China’s capital formation now came from and would “continue to be driven by state-backed programmes and corporate ecosystems”, as nondomestic participation remained limited, said Melanie Tng, an analyst at PitchBook focusing on Asia-Pacific private capital, in a note this week.


“In China, the combination of provincial subsidies and corporate initiatives from … [Big Tech] firms continue to support large-scale AI commercialisation, even as private [venture capital] liquidity tightens,” Tng said.
Alibaba Group Holding and Tencent Holdings have emerged as two of the country’s largest investors in AI, backing high-flyers from Beijing-based Moonshot AI to Shanghai-headquartered MiniMax. State-backed investment vehicles, meanwhile, have been writing cheques for companies like Zhipu, also known as Z.ai. Alibaba owns the Post.
Rather than a dearth of capital, China lacked a merit-based and transparent financial system, which could discourage investors from making big AI bets in the country, said Su Lian Jye, chief analyst at consulting firm Omdia.
Chinese investors were making more deals with smaller basket sizes, according to data compiled by local venture capital analytics firm ITJuzi.
They backed 741 AI-related start-ups as of early December, which is a 40 per cent jump from 521 companies in 2024, the data showed. However, the investment value stayed consistent at around 62 billion yuan (US$8.6 billion) in both time frames, indicating that investors were spreading their bets across a broader portfolio, as they anticipated real-world AI applications in traditional sectors.
In his Monday talk, Schmidt said that China was “focusing on embedding AI into everything” from smart toasters to cars to robots, thanks to the country’s advantages in manufacturing, supply chains and cost management.
The gap between China and the US in AI would widen for various reasons, including the funding crunch, Schmidt said, while also acknowledging China’s strength in open-source AI.
“I don’t see the open-source leadership in America, and I see nothing but open-source leadership in China,” he said. “I think those are the facts.”

data.title: UK report blames Putin for ‘reckless’ 2018 Novichok poisoning of ex-Russian spy Skripal
data.description:
Russian President Vladimir Putin must have ordered the Novichok nerve agent attack on Russian double agent Sergei Skripal in 2018, in an “astonishingly reckless” act that led to the death of an innocent woman, a UK public inquiry concluded on Thursday.
Skripal was found along with his daughter Yulia slumped unconscious on a public bench in the southern English city of Salisbury in March 2018 after Novichok was applied to the front door handle of his nearby home.
About four months later, mother-of-three Dawn Sturgess, 44, died from exposure to the poison after her partner found a counterfeit perfume bottle which Russian spies had used to smuggle the military-grade nerve agent into the country, the inquiry said.

Overwhelming evidence
The Skripals, and a police officer who went to Skripal’s house, were left critically ill from its effects, but recovered.
In his conclusions, the chair, former UK Supreme Court judge Anthony Hughes, said he was certain a team of GRU military intelligence officers had carried out the murder attempt on Skripal, who sold Russian secrets to Britain and moved there after a 2010 spy swap.
“I have concluded that the operation to assassinate Sergei Skripal must have been authorised at the highest level, by President Putin,” Hughes said in his report.
“The evidence that this was a Russian state attack is overwhelming.”

As a result, Britain sanctioned Russia’s GRU intelligence agency and summoned Moscow’s ambassador on Thursday.
Russia has always denied any involvement, casting the accusations as anti-Russian propaganda. The Russian embassy in London did not respond immediately to a request for comment.
Hughes said the “astonishingly reckless” actions meant the would-be assassins, their GRU superiors and those who authorised the attack, up to Putin himself, bore moral responsibility for Sturgess’ death.

Although Putin had previously denounced Skripal as a traitor, the inquiry said there was nothing to suggest the double agent had been imminently at risk.
It found there had been failings in how Skripal was managed as a double agent, but did not conclude the attack could have been prevented without taking the dramatic security measures of hiding him entirely with a new identity and no family contact – something he himself did not want.

Thursday’s report is the second major investigation to blame Putin for attacks on British soil against his perceived enemies.
An inquiry in 2016 concluded that Putin had probably ordered the murder in London of Alexander Litvinenko, a Russian dissident and former agent of the FSB security service, using radioactive polonium-210.
Additional reporting by Associated Press

data.title: Winners of AI spending mania won’t necessarily be the innovators
data.description:
Drilling down on the artificial intelligence (AI) investment story is becoming critical as stock markets find excuses for ever greater enthusiasm. Any little story with an AI theme is driving up stock prices, assisted by the billions pledged for the building of data centres to handle all of our future searches.
The share price of Fanuc, a Japanese robotic company, jumped 9 per cent on the opening bell on Tuesday after it announced a tie-up with AI darling Nvidia. AI is likely to be a great tool, but the market is currently borrowing performance, revenues and profits from the future, much the same as it did in the dotcom bubble of 2000.
AI is under heavy scrutiny at the moment, partly because people are looking ahead and seeing a time when many jobs will disappear. We can look to the history of the Industrial Revolution and see that some new roles are likely to appear after the short-term pain. Indeed, some companies are already trying to shed labour even though AI customer service is barely a work in progress.
So-called virtual assistants are slow and error-strewn. Apps are often difficult to use with intractable logic for anyone older than 15. Programmers focus their efforts on the sales part, but it is nearly impossible to find somebody if you need after-sales help. If you are lucky enough to find a contact number, you are likely to be told that the information can be found on the website. If I could find it on the website, I wouldn’t have called.
This form of AI is fundamentally a clever form of predictive text that takes the search concept and uses probabilities to forecast what is likely to come next, presenting it as fact. However, the human mind is still better at discerning the nuances of a topic and will be for decades.
With due modesty (because I know the answers), I tested the different AI apps by using the search string “Richard Harris Hong Kong”. I received a wide range of answers, mostly with errors and one or two being definitely untrue, sometimes dangerously so. AI might be useful in writing a novel or screenplay, but you would not want it to write a PhD thesis, a legal case or commentary on the news.


I have three observations about AI. The first is recognisable for those who use it to manipulate video: don’t believe anything you see on video. That includes YouTube, which unfortunately is a source of news for many people and threatens to overwhelm mainstream media, where there is a tradition of verifying news before it is published.
AI companies are mining our personal data with the inevitable logic being that they will feed our own biases back to us. It makes the echo chamber of the internet smaller and louder, further distorting the truth. AI is smart enough to give an elegant answer, but the assumptions on which the answer was based are obscure.
The second is that many friends and colleagues who are people of legacy education, training, skill, sophistication and experience find AI searches useful. However, that is only because they are smart enough to discern the positives and negatives of the answers provided by AI. They are merely adding another tool to their bag of skills. This is artificial data collection rather than artificial intelligence.

The third observation is that AI in the short term is likely to be most useful at a low level, as it has been in efforts to optimise the likes of automatic cameras and autonomous landing technology in aircraft.
The most immediate benefit is in the Internet of Things, such as controlling factory equipment, robots, closed circuit cameras or other mechanical devices, where such equipment can increase productivity or take the right action in the event of a problem. Chinese robotics inventors are on the leading edge of this technology, although I wonder why robots still have to look like humanoids and not have more than two arms.
There is not enough room in the market for all of the competitors raising billions of dollars to invest in AI. There will be more losers than winners. The likes of Apple, Google, Microsoft and Meta are looking to drive their platforms off their own competitive advantages.
Apple is founded on easy-to-use hardware and software, Microsoft on business software and the cloud. Meta has a massive retail following and brand presence. China is already a significant player with many companies, the most famous being DeepSeek. Europe, India and Israel have their own champions but could struggle to find the scale to survive.


The secret to success in a competitive environment is mass adoption, and the secret to mass adoption is distribution. If you put something in front of everybody, they are more likely to use it.
My forecast is that the existing companies that are well positioned to benefit from its natural advantages will gobble up their less successful competitors. Users are likely to stick to their current favourite platforms, run by companies which have the advantage in terms of size and distribution.
In the virtual world, all AI engines will be good, but only a few will survive. The experience of Betamax and Lotus 1-2-3 is testimony to the fact that the innovators often don’t survive to profitability but are overwhelmed by those who happen to be in the right place.

data.title: Tai Po players ‘here to help’ community as they play first match in Hong Kong since fire
data.description:
Tai Po had to settle for a 1-1 draw from an absorbing encounter with North District, as they returned to domestic action on Thursday following the fire that killed at least 159 people in the district.
Philip Chan Siu-kwan turned home a 51st-minute cross from Fernando to give Tai Po the lead, although the goal was awarded only after a five-minute VAR check for offside.
Almost immediately after play resumed, Weverton Gudula strode onto a Yilber Arboleda pass to place an equalising strike across Tse Ka-wing.
All ticket proceeds from the match were donated to the Wang Fuk Court support fund.
Ngan Cheuk-pan, who excelled out of position at right-back for Tai Po, said: “It was hard to focus on football again … we’re a district team and some of the fans and their families live [in the affected area].

“We hope we provided 90 minutes for them to get away from this tragedy.
“We’re here to help them, and happy to open our messages for anybody who wants to talk.”
In an opening half largely dominated by the visitors, North District keeper Li Hon-ho touched round a James Temelkovski curler, thwarted Igor Sartori one-on-one and frustrated Patrick Valverde after the Brazilian aimed a free-kick towards the top corner.
Influential Tai Po defender Gabriel Cividini hobbled off injured, after clattering the post from a ninth-minute Sartori corner.
“We have so many games, you know the schedule of Hong Kong football … but it’s OK,” said Lee Chi-kin, the Tai Po boss, who has lived in the district for more than 25 years.
“I know a lot of people there, it’s not easy for anybody,” Lee said. “We have no choice but to carry on – this is our job.”
The usually clinical Samuel Granada, the division’s top marksman with seven goals, lifted North District’s first clear-cut chance wastefully over.
Chan’s goal for Tai Po was his first since the opening day of the campaign, although he endured a long wait to discover if it would count.
Lee said: “I don’t know what happened, I never believe what’s going on. I hope [VAR] can improve more.”
North District stayed second, four points behind Kitchee, with Tai Po, who go to Football Club on Sunday, one point and one place behind.
Kitchee, meanwhile, revealed that their ticket and merchandise sales from Tuesday’s meeting with Eastern District had raised HK$718,416.60 (US$92,300) for Tai Po funds.

data.title: Gucci, Prada, Versace face Italy probe over alleged Chinese labour exploitation
data.description:
Italian prosecutors on Thursday named Gucci, Prada, Versace and Yves Saint Laurent as among 13 luxury brands suspected of using subcontractors who exploited migrant workers in Italy, as part of a growing investigation into sweatshop conditions.
Milan prosecutor Paolo Storari issued requests for information about alleged abuses by the brands, although they are not yet under formal investigation.
It is the biggest expansion to date of a probe launched last year into the luxury sector, which has exposed wage and working hour violations, safety breaches and substandard housing for staff.
“As part of the investigations … there have been reports of Chinese workers being employed in conditions of severe exploitation,” Storari wrote to the brands in documents made public on Thursday.
The document reveals that luxury bags, wallets and clothing were found during searches of Italian workshops employing Chinese and Pakistani workers under exploitative conditions.
It requests information including internal governance documents and audit information related to the brands’ supply chains.
The documents were sent to some of the fashion industry’s high-flyers, from Gucci, Yves Saint Laurent and Alexander McQueen – which form part of the French luxury group Kering – to Givenchy, which is part of the French giant LVMH.
Prosecutors also named Italian brands Prada and its new acquisition, Versace, as well as Ferragamo, Pinko, Dolce & Gabbana, Missoni, Off-White, Coccinelle and German sportswear giant Adidas.
None of the brands immediately replied to requests for comment.

Inspections
Milan prosecutors have already launched investigations against Tod’s, Loro Piana, Dior’s Italian subsidiary Manufactures Dior, Giorgio Armani Operations, Alviero Martini and Valentino Bags Lab.
All but Tod’s were placed under temporary court administration to correct compliance issues and establish systems to avoid future abuses.
On Wednesday, a Milan judge allowed Tod’s more time to complete an evaluation of their supply chain controls as prosecutors seek to impose a temporary advertising ban and outside administrators.
The probes have cast a spotlight on the near-ubiquitous practice of luxury brands subcontracting work to suppliers, who in turn contract to others, amid ever-tighter margins and scant oversight of labour conditions.
Under Italian law, companies can be held responsible for offences committed by representatives – such as approved suppliers – acting in their interest.
Italy’s government has gone on the offensive, with Industry Minister Adolfo Urso saying the reputation of “Made in Italy” brands was “under attack”.

Campaigners against sweatshops, however, say, the brands themselves impose prices on their contractors that are too low, driving them to subcontract to second- and third-tier suppliers where oversight is lacking.
Deborah Lucchetti, Italy’s coordinator for the Clean Clothes Campaign, called worker exploitation a “structural phenomenon” within the sector.
“Made in Italy cannot be a stage on which to celebrate astronomical profits built on the denial of dignity to those who sew, assemble, and finish products,” she said in a statement Thursday.
The request for information from prosecutors follows inspections at five suppliers used by multiple brands, as recently as November.
Between three and 19 workers, most of them Chinese but also Pakistani, worked at each supplier and were named in the documents.
Some of the brands targeted are the parent companies’ Italian subsidiaries, such as Yves Saint Laurent Manifatture, Alexander McQueen Italia and Givenchy Italia.

data.title: Give pupils, parents say on cancelling Christmas parties, Hong Kong schools told
data.description:
Hong Kong education authorities have urged schools to seek a consensus with pupils and parents on whether to cancel Christmas celebrations following the Tai Po inferno, with some students expressing disappointment on social media about parties being called off.
The Education Bureau’s clarification on Thursday followed a letter it sent last week asking schools to mourn the victims of the fire, which has now claimed 159 lives, and suspended festive celebratory activities from November 29 to December 1.
One social media platform was flooded with messages from students who said they found it unnecessary to suspend all school activities as they had spent a lot of time preparing and had been looking forward to the annual events.
“Why [do we] have to cancel the Christmas parties? [We] prepare for a long time. Of course I know the fire in Tai Po is serious, but could it not cancel our Christmas party?” a user named “fsj_0502” wrote in Threads, with tens of thousands of internet users echoing that view and retweeting the post.


Some said that they also had their Christmas parties cancelled, and what disappointed them the most was that it was their last year at the school.
“This is our last Christmas in our school. Now it has to be cancelled. Actually we prayed and we donated our money, we do what we can all do. I do not understand why they do this to us,” a user named “zokawen” wrote.
“Why must schools cancel Christmas parties? The only chance [for students] to have a sign of relief is going to school but it has to be eliminated. Shifting your attention from distressing feelings to a different, engaging activity to make a situation more tolerable,” she said.
Some said the city should move on while still helping those in need after the blaze, which swept through seven of the eight residential blocks at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po on November 26 and raged for more than 40 hours.
“Everyone deserves to move on in their daily life, people can feel relaxed and happy after joining such activities while we all still help those affected by the fire,” a user named “Pumawong” said.
Another user said annual parties could help ease the pressure on students, as everyone had been feeling low.
Some also complained about student activities being cancelled while the Legislative Council election would go ahead as scheduled on Sunday.




Last Friday, the bureau sent a letter to schools reminding them to suspend all celebratory activities and performances while the city officially mourned the fire victims from November 29 to December 1.
The letter did not specifically ask the schools to cancel Christmas celebrations.
A bureau spokeswoman said on Thursday that schools had to carefully consider its situation and secure a consensus from students and parents before adjusting activities.
“Regarding activities after December 1, it is understood that some schools have made adjustments in response to the fire in Tai Po. For example, Christmas activities have been themed as caring and cherishing, with fewer celebratory elements and some activities being postponed,” she said.
“The Education Bureau reminds schools to carefully consider their specific circumstances and maintain good communication and obtain consensus from students and parents before making any adjustments to their activities arrangements.”
